label: Los Pinos — Presidential Residence
sub: Seat of executive power from which Calderón directed the drug war strategy and received foreign dignitaries including President Obama. Site of key Mérida Initiative negotiations.

label: Morelia — Operation Michoacán HQ
sub: State capital where Calderón launched the drug war on Dec 11, 2006, deploying 6,500 soldiers. Also site of a deadly grenade attack on an Independence Day crowd in September 2008 that killed 8 civilians.

label: Ciudad Juárez — World's Most Violent City
sub: Epicenter of the Sinaloa vs. Juárez Cartel turf war. Over 3,111 murders in 2010 alone made it the world's most dangerous city. Calderón deployed 10,000+ troops here at peak.

label: Culiacán — Sinaloa Cartel Headquarters
sub: Home base of Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán and the Sinaloa Cartel, the dominant drug trafficking organization of the era. Site of the 2008 assassination of El Chapo's son Edgar Guzmán López.

label: Cuernavaca — Arturo Beltrán Leyva Killed
sub: Mexican Marines killed BLO boss Arturo Beltrán Leyva 'El Jefe de Jefes' at a luxury apartment complex here on December 16, 2009, in a four-hour firefight guided by US intelligence.

label: San Fernando — 72 Migrants Massacred
sub: Site of the August 2010 San Fernando massacre where Los Zetas executed 72 Central and South American migrants on a ranch. Also location of 2011 mass graves containing 193 more victims.

label: Monterrey — Casino Royale Attack (52 Killed)
sub: Los Zetas firebombed Casino Royale on August 25, 2011, killing 52 people — mostly women playing bingo — because the casino refused to pay extortion. Calderón declared three days of national mourning.

label: Matamoros — Gulf Cartel Stronghold
sub: Principal base of the Gulf Cartel, where Tony Tormenta (Antonio Cárdenas Guillén) was killed in a major marine operation in November 2010. Site of intense Gulf-Zetas violence after their 2010 split.

label: Tijuana — Arellano Félix Cartel Zone
sub: Territory of the Tijuana Cartel (AFO), scene of extreme violence 2008–2009 during the Sinaloa-AFO war. Violence declined sharply after El Teo's capture and Sinaloa's consolidation of control by 2010.

label: Nuevo Laredo — Zetas Original Stronghold
sub: Strategic border city that Los Zetas controlled as the Gulf Cartel's armed wing and retained after the 2010 split. One of Mexico's busiest drug trafficking corridors into Texas, fought over throughout the drug war.

label: Zapopan — Nacho Coronel Killed July 2010
sub: Mexican Army forces killed Ignacio 'Nacho' Coronel, Sinaloa Cartel's 'Crystal King' and methamphetamine lord, in a private residence in Zapopan, Jalisco on July 29, 2010.

label: Mérida — Birthplace of the Mérida Initiative
sub: City where President Calderón and President Bush first agreed in March 2007 on the framework for the bilateral security cooperation package that became the Mérida Initiative.

label: Lázaro Cárdenas Port — Drug Import Hub
sub: Major Pacific port controlled at various points by La Familia Michoacana and later the Knights Templar for importing precursor chemicals from Asia used in methamphetamine production. A key economic asset for cartel operations.

label: Reynosa — Gulf-Zetas Battleground
sub: Border city adjacent to McAllen, Texas, site of intense Gulf Cartel vs. Zetas fighting after the 2010 split. A strategic plaza for the Tamaulipas drug corridor into the United States.

label: Acapulco — Pacific Resort Turned Warzone
sub: Once a premier tourist resort, Acapulco became one of Mexico's most violent cities during the Calderón era as multiple cartels fought for control of the port and Pacific trafficking routes. The city recorded among the world's highest per-capita murder rates by 2012.

label: Chihuahua City — State Government Under Siege
sub: State capital of Chihuahua, which suffered the highest total homicide count of any state during the Calderón era. Governor José Reyes Baeza faced constant pressure from cartel violence that overwhelmed municipal police forces.

label: Hermosillo, Sonora — BLO Territory
sub: Capital of Sonora state and base of operations for the Beltrán Leyva Organization, which controlled access to the Arizona border before splitting from Sinaloa in 2008. Site of significant cartel violence during the BLO-Sinaloa war.

label: Nogales — Sonora/Arizona Drug Corridor
sub: One of Mexico's busiest drug trafficking corridors into the United States, controlled primarily by the Sinaloa Cartel. Site of numerous tunnel discoveries by US and Mexican authorities during the Calderón era.

label: Veracruz — Zetas Expand to Gulf Coast
sub: Port city and state capital where Los Zetas expanded aggressively after their 2010 split with the Gulf Cartel. In 2011, 35 bodies were dumped on a major highway in broad daylight, attributed to the Zetas.

label: Manzanillo, Colima — Pacific Drug Port
sub: Mexico's busiest Pacific commercial port and a major entry point for cocaine shipments from South America and precursor chemicals from Asia used in drug manufacturing by multiple cartels.

label: Saltillo — Zetas Territorial Control in Coahuila
sub: Capital of Coahuila, a state where the Zetas had deep political connections including with Governor Humberto Moreira (later arrested for corruption). The body of Zetas founder Heriberto Lazcano was stolen from a Saltillo funeral home in October 2012.

label: Durango — Golden Triangle Drug Production
sub: Part of the 'Golden Triangle' drug production zone spanning Durango, Sinaloa, and Chihuahua. Major area for marijuana and opium cultivation. Numerous mass graves were discovered in Durango during 2011, containing hundreds of victims.

label: Uruapan — La Familia Michoacana Stronghold
sub: City where La Familia Michoacana announced its existence in 2006 by rolling five severed heads onto a disco dance floor with a note reading 'The Family doesn't kill for money, it kills for justice.' One of the most brazen cartel declarations in Mexico's history.

label: Chapultepec Castle — Calderón–Sicilia Peace Dialogue
sub: Historic site where President Calderón met poet-activist Javier Sicilia and thousands of peace movement members on June 23, 2011, for an unprecedented nationally televised public dialogue about the drug war's human cost.

label: La Paz, BCS — 'El Teo' Captured
sub: Federal police captured Teodoro 'El Teo' García Simental — Tijuana Cartel splinter leader notorious for dissolving rivals in acid — in La Paz, Baja California Sur, on January 12, 2010.

label: Agua Prieta — Historic El Chapo Sinaloa Corridor
sub: Sonoran border town opposite Douglas, Arizona. One of El Chapo Guzmán's original drug smuggling bases, known for elaborate tunnel networks. The Sinaloa Cartel fiercely defended this corridor throughout the drug war.

label: Tamaulipas — Zetas Home Territory
sub: Northeastern border state that served as the original base of operations for Los Zetas. After their 2010 split with the Gulf Cartel, Tamaulipas became a battleground between the two groups, accounting for a disproportionate share of Mexico's drug war deaths.

label: Progreso, Coahuila — Lazcano Killed (Oct 2012)
sub: Small Coahuila town where Mexican Marines killed Heriberto 'El Lazca' Lazcano — co-founder and leader of Los Zetas — in a confrontation on October 7, 2012, days before Calderón's term ended.

label: State of Mexico — 'La Barbie' Captured
sub: Federal police apprehended American-born BLO commander Edgar Valdez Villarreal 'La Barbie' without a shot fired in the State of Mexico on August 30, 2010 — one of Calderón's highest-profile captures.

label: Mazatlán — Sinaloa Cartel Pacific Port
sub: Major Pacific port city dominated by the Sinaloa Cartel, used for cocaine shipments and maintaining internal cartel operations. El Chapo was eventually captured in Mazatlán by Mexican marines and DEA in February 2014.

label: Oaxaca — Southern Drug Trafficking Corridor
sub: State through which South American cocaine is transited northward, with various cartels competing for control. Also site of significant social unrest during the 2006 APPO protests that coincided with Calderón's contested election.

label: Campo Militar No. 1 — SEDENA Headquarters
sub: Mexico City's main military base from which Defense Secretary Guillermo Galván Galván coordinated the nationwide deployment of 96,000 troops against drug cartels. Also site of allegations of detainee torture by human rights organizations.

label: Piedras Negras — Mass Prison Break (131 Zetas Escape)
sub: Site of one of Mexico's largest prison escapes on September 17, 2012, when 131 Zetas members walked out through the front door with apparent guard complicity, exposing catastrophic corruption in Coahuila's prison system.

label: Tabasco — Marine's Family Assassinated in Retaliation
sub: BLO gunmen killed the mother, sister, and two brothers of Marine Melquisedec Angulo Córdova in Tabasco on December 22, 2009 — six days after the marine was killed during the operation that took down Arturo Beltrán Leyva.

label: Cancún — Cartel Money Laundering Hub
sub: Resort city where multiple cartels laundered drug proceeds through tourism, real estate, and restaurant businesses. The Caribbean coast corridor to the US and Europe made Cancún a financial hub for criminal organizations.

label: Ciudad Mante, Tamaulipas — Zetas Extortion Zone
sub: Small Tamaulipas city representative of the Zetas' 'piso' extortion model — taxing all commerce and criminality in their territory. Businesses paid weekly fees or faced violence, generating revenues parallel to drug trafficking.

label: Villas de Salvárcar — 16 Students Massacred (Jan 2010)
sub: Working-class neighborhood in Ciudad Juárez where gunmen massacred 16 teenagers at a birthday party on January 31, 2010. Calderón initially dismissed the victims as gang members while in Davos, triggering national outrage.

label: Guerrero State — La Familia / BLO Territory
sub: Pacific state with one of the highest homicide rates in Mexico during the Calderón era. Shared between La Familia Michoacana and BLO, with Acapulco as the main flashpoint of cartel violence and femicide.

label: U.S. Embassy, Mexico City — Mérida Initiative Hub
sub: The primary coordination point for US-Mexico security cooperation, where Mérida Initiative deliverables were tracked, DEA operations coordinated, and State Department human rights reports on Mexico compiled throughout the Calderón years.

label: Tecate — Baja California Border Drug Crossing
sub: Small Baja California border crossing used by the Tijuana Cartel as a secondary corridor when El Chapo's Sinaloa forces contested the main Tijuana crossing. Captured in SEDENA operations during 2008–2009.

label: Sinaloa Sierra — Golden Triangle Drug Production Zone
sub: Mountainous 'Golden Triangle' region spanning Sinaloa, Durango, and Chihuahua — Mexico's primary marijuana and opium poppy cultivation area. Effectively controlled by the Sinaloa Cartel throughout the Calderón era, inaccessible to army patrols.

label: Fresnillo, Zacatecas — Cartel Crossroads
sub: Zacatecas city where Edgar 'El Wache' Huerta Montiel — key Zetas lieutenant responsible for the San Fernando massacre — was captured by federal police on June 17, 2011.
